title: Clove and orange pomander
subtitle: A traditional studded orange pomander for a wardrobe or drawers
excerpt: An orange studded with whole cloves and rolled in ground cinnamon and orris root powder. Cloves are a natural moth repellent and powerful room fragrance. Orris root powder acts as a fixative that extends the life of the fragrance. The pomander dries naturally and lasts for years in a wardrobe.
sourceNotes: Traditional clove pomander technique from Constance Spry, Constance Spry Cookery Book (1956). Orris root as fragrance fixative from Arctander, Perfume and Flavor Materials of Natural Origin (1960). Clove moth repellent properties from the Royal Entomological Society guidance.

recipe.batchNotes: Make several pomanders at once: the curing time is the same regardless of quantity, and they make excellent gifts.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 to 3 weeks for the pomander to dry fully. A fully dried pomander is hard, lightweight, and will last several years. A pomander that is still moist inside will eventually grow mould.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 years or more once fully dried. The clove fragrance slowly fades over 2 to 3 years. When the fragrance fades, add 5 drops of clove bud essential oil to the surface to refresh. Store in a wardrobe, drawer, or open bowl.

glossaryTerms.definition: Powdered dried rhizome of Iris germanica used as a traditional fragrance fixative. The orris root powder binds with and slows the evaporation of the volatile fragrance molecules from the cloves and orange peel, extending the life of the pomander. It has a faint violet-like scent of its own.

content.text: A pomander is one of the oldest fragrance traditions in Britain. The word comes from the French pomme d'ambre: apple of amber. The original pomanders were hollow gold or silver balls filled with ambergris. The orange-and-clove version became popular in Tudor England as a cheaper way to freshen musty wardrobes. Cloves contain eugenol, a natural insect repellent. The dried orange peel provides a long-lasting citrus base.

Use a thick-skinned orange: thin-skinned varieties bruise easily and the skin splits when the cloves are pushed in. A skewer or cocktail stick makes clean holes that reduce the effort needed to push each clove in. Space the cloves evenly across the whole surface; gaps cause uneven drying and can lead to mould developing in the less-dried sections.
and ground cinnamon extend the fragrance life significantly: do not omit them.

items.prepNote: A large, thick-skinned orange; firm and unblemished
Whole cloves; enough to cover the entire orange surface
Combined with orris root for the rolling mixture
Fragrance fixative; combined with ground cinnamon for rolling

content.text: Use a cocktail stick or skewer to make small holes across the surface of the orange, spaced approximately 5 mm apart. Push a whole clove into each hole, keeping the stud flush with the skin surface. Cover the entire orange including the base. This takes 30 to 45 minutes.

Combine the ground cinnamon and orris root powder in a bowl. Roll the studded orange in the powder mixture, pressing gently to coat the skin and the gaps between cloves. Place the coated pomander in a paper bag or loosely wrapped in tissue paper. Store in a cool, dry place with good air circulation for 2 to 3 weeks to dry. Turn the pomander every few days during drying.

3 years or more once fully dried and hardened. The pomander is ready when it feels significantly lighter and completely firm throughout. Hang in a wardrobe with ribbon or place in a drawer. The clove fragrance deters moths and refreshes the air. Refresh with 5 drops of clove bud essential oil when the scent fades.
